driving_event_text: DSN is a set of large antennas and communication facilities, managed by the NASA/Caltech Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) and located at Goldstone (California), near Madrid (Spain), and near Canberra (Australia), that provide uplink and downlink services to deep space missions. The geographical distribution of the three antenna complexes around the globe permits continuous communications between the ground and the spacecraft as the Earth revolves. On August 21, 2011, a lightning strike to earth approximately 150 to 200 meters from the DSS-54 34-meter antenna (Figure 1) at the MDSCC caused extensive equipment damage. A total of 34 items related to DSS-54 and DSS-55, including various circuit boards, modules, controllers, and assemblies related to antenna positioning, monitoring, power processing, and facility infrastructure suffered damage from the strike (References (1) and (2)). Figure 1. Madrid DSS-54 antenna The four antennas at the MDSCC each incorporate protection against lightning strikes. However, the facility grounding system and surge protection proved inadequate for the severity of the lightning storm (Reference (2)). No personnel were injured because MDSCC personnel are not allowed outdoors in the vicinity of antennas when lightning is forecast. The total damage was estimated at $58,000. The damage had a direct impact on DSN operations, affecting the JPL DAWN project and others: DAWN lost 180 minutes of command capability and lost 141 minutes of telemetry data. THEMIS-B lost 22 minutes of telemetry data and 22 minutes of radiometric data. GEOTAIL lost 60 minutes of telemetry data. STEREO-A lost 244 minutes of command capability, 250 minutes of telemetry data, and 244 minutes of radiometric data. MESSENGER lost 26 minutes of radiometric data. Following the incident, DSN embarked on repairs to damaged equipment-- and on facility improvements to correct the deficiencies reported in Reference (2). As one example of an identified deficiency, although the DSS-54 Antenna apex was well grounded, it was found that the North Camera Pole (Figure 2) did not have its base (Figure 3) grounded to the DSS-54 Antenna buried ground ring. Most of the MDSCC improvements-- intended to decrease facility lightning susceptibility-- have now been completed, and the lessons learned are being flowed to the other two DSN complexes. Figure 2. North Camera Pole located outside the access control (inner) fence Figure 3. Base of the North Camera Pole References: MRS No. 2487, JPL Mishap Reporting System, August 23, 2011. Site Inspection Report, NASA DSS-54 Antenna Madrid Deep Space Communications Complex, September 18, 2015.
lesson_text: Providing full facility protection against naturally occurring events (e.g., hurricane, lightning, tornado, high winds, dust storm, tidal wave, tsunami, waterspout, ice/snow loads, flood, landslide, earthquake, volcanic eruption, or wildfire) may not be immediately viewed as justified where the cost exceeds the likely damage. However, the potential for such damage to interrupt irretrievable downlinked science return or prevent timely uplinked spacecraft commanding must also be considered.
recommendation_text: In evaluating candidate measures to protect NASA facilities from naturally occurring events, consider not only the cost of facility repair under likely scenarios, but also consider the impacts that temporary loss of facility function may have on science return, spacecraft commanding, and other functions critical to NASA missions.

evidence.quote: the facility grounding system and surge protection proved inadequate for the severity of the lightning storm
the North Camera Pole did not have its base grounded to the DSS-54 Antenna buried ground ring
the potential for such damage to interrupt irretrievable downlinked science return or prevent timely uplinked spacecraft commanding must also be considered

impact_notes: $58,000 equipment damage; multiple missions lost critical command/telemetry windows (DAWN 180 min command, STEREO-A 244 min command, combined 1,109 minutes of science data loss across 6 missions)

title: Thorough Assessment of Facility Condition During Project Planning and Budgeting
program_relation: Ground Systems Development and Operations (GSDO) Program
abstract_text: Reuse of heritage facilities and equipment can save Programs and Projects substantial amounts of money but there is a risk that heritage systems can cause substantial budget and schedule impacts if their condition is not thoroughly assessed and required upgrades planned into the project budget and schedule. It is strongly recommended that a thorough condition assessment be conducted and documented during project formulation and that the assessments be updated if the schedule for bringing the systems online changes substantially.
driving_event_text: During the design and development phase of the Ground Systems Development and Operations (GSDO) Program there were a number of instances where unexpected costs and schedule delays resulted from discovering the following types of unexpected issues: Lead-based paint, the presence of asbestos, discovery of structural inadequacy of existing systems, and the discovery of hidden corrosion. Specific driving events: 1. During refurbishment of Kennedy Space Center Launch Pad 39B, unexpected corrosion was found in various locations including inside the water tank for the Pad water deluge system. This water tank is very large and the corrosion was a major impact. 2. During modifications to the work platforms in the Rotation Processing and Surge Facility (RPSF) Discovery of Lead-based paint resulted in schedule delays and cost increase Discovery of inadequacies in existing platforms during implementation of modifications resulted in cost and schedule impacts due to repair and reinforcement Unplanned disassembly of decking was required in order to paint the platforms 3. During modification of the Crawler Transporter, asbestos was found in the control room ceiling. 4. During the Vehicle Assembly Building refurbishment, the condition of elevator motors and Environmental Control System supply piping were not understood during budget estimation
lesson_text: Programs and Projects can be substantially impacted by unexpected additional work resulting from unforeseen issues with reuse of heritage facilities and systems. Thorough assessments of heritage systems need to be performed early, updated as required during the project life cycle and used as a Basis of Estimate for budget and schedule. The assessments should include thorough inspections and analyses.
recommendation_text: Program/Project Managers relying on the use of heritage hardware, facilities and infrastructure should perform a thorough assessment of the capabilities and condition of these assets early in the Program/Project. Assessments should consider the following: a) The extent of rework required to comply with changes in codes and regulations (OSHA, National Electrical Code, Pressure Vessel System Code, etc.) b) Existing issues that will require mitigation; e.g., corrosion, environmental remediation, the presence of asbestos or lead based paint in facilities c) Supportability and maintainability analyses, considering issues such as parts obsolescence. d) The assessment should document all assumptions related to facility conditions, required maintenance, rework and supportability of facility systems (supportability of older systems is sensitive to project delays as obtaining repair parts becomes more difficult over time) e) For systems including software, consideration should be given to the computing platform, operating system and maintaining of any COTS or custom applications f) Address time sensitivity of this analysis (if the project incurs significant delays how the assessment will be changed) 3. The condition assessment should include physical inspections of visible as well as hard-to-access areas (e.g. hidden corrosion inside a tank or metal structure) 4. The assessment should be updated in the event that the need date for the heritage systems slips as this may invalidate the previous analysis

evidence.quote: unexpected costs and schedule delays resulted from discovering...Lead-based paint, asbestos, structural inadequacy of existing systems, and hidden corrosion
Thorough assessments of heritage systems need to be performed early, updated as required during the project life cycle and used as a Basis of Estimate for budget and schedule
The condition assessment should include physical inspections of visible as well as hard-to-access areas (e.g. hidden corrosion inside a tank or metal structure)

impact_notes: Multiple heritage facility projects experienced substantial budget overruns and schedule delays due to undiscovered corrosion, hazardous materials (lead paint, asbestos), and structural inadequacies discovered during implementation rather than planning phase.

title: Facility Test Set-Up Not Fully Inspected Prior To Use / Heritage Panels Unintentionally Pressurized
abstract_text: The Ground Systems Development and Operations (GSDO) Program was modifying subsystems in the Multi Payload Processing Facility (MPPF) at the Kennedy Space Center (KSC) to support processing of Exploration Mission 1 flight hardware. The modifications at the MPPF included the facility Gaseous Nitrogen (GN2) system. On August 16 th , 2016 a pressurization test of the GN2 system was conducted. The intent of the test was to pressurize the new or modified parts of the GN2 system as part of Verification and Validation (V&V) but four panels that were part of the unmodified heritage portion of the GN2 system were also inadvertently pressurized. Had there been any disconnected lines or leaks in the tubing assemblies, this condition could have caused an oxygen deficient atmosphere within the interstitial walls of the facility and /or the high bay and airlock areas. Engineering, the Systems Engineers and the NASA Chief Engineers agreed to classify this event as a “Close Call”. The Close Call was caused by inadequate understanding of the actual configuration of the system under test and communication of this organization between the organizations involved in system design and testing. The Test Readiness Review (TRR) did not adequately address the configuration of the system under test. Communication between multiple organizations working on the same facility is imperative. Test Readiness Reviews need to question test/system configuration to ensure cross checking between drawings applicable to systems being tested, facility drawings, and other organizations
driving_event_text: During the course of GN2 V&V operations in the MPPF, personnel noted heritage panels labeled as “Gaseous Outlet Panels”. One such panel, located on the south wall had been previously opened and remained open enough to see inside, all of the gaseous lines were clearly capped/terminated, leading personnel to assume that all 6 of the similar GN2 panels shared the same configuration. After testing was initiated and facility GN2 pressure was applied, another cabinet was opened and it was observed to still have valves, regulators, gages, etc. installed for both the GN2 and Gaseous Helium (Ghe) systems and that pressure was present. Further investigation identified that 4 of the 6 heritage panels had valves, regulators, and gages rather than capped lines. Following discussions with GN2 Systems Engineers, Lead Design Engineers, Facility representatives, and Chief Engineers, the V&V work was halted until the panels could be more thoroughly evaluated and placed into a safe configuration. KSC utilizes different organizations to operate and maintain different types of systems (e.g. Information Technology systems, facilities and facility systems, or Ground Support Equipment). The GSDO Program also used various organizations to do facility modifications and Ground Support Equipment. The situation is also complex because of the mixture of unmodified heritage systems and new/modified systems. The MPPF facility GN2 system is partly unmodified heritage and partly new/modified. The drawings for the heritage part of the system were created and maintained by an organization different than that designing and implementing the new/modified part of the system. This leads to multiple organizations with responsibilities in the same facility and different groups may be modifying or maintaining different parts of the same system. Facility modification drawing (245G2200002) does not consistently identify supply lines, and the interior layout of the panels was unclear.
lesson_text: Communication between multiple organizations working on the same facility is imperative. Test Readiness Reviews must thoroughly address the configuration of the system under test to ensure cross checking between different organizational drawings applicable to systems being tested and facility drawings.
recommendation_text: Verify all panels not part of testing are capped and plugged. The capped lines need to be labeled with commodity and pressure. Conduct a walk downs of entire system configurations prior to Test Readiness Reviews (TRR) as well as two mandatory questions to answer at TRR: For the configuration under test, are you only pressurizing/energizing the items/systems under test? Did you cross check with other documents/drawings and other organizations? Communication: Team communication is key to safety. All parties need to be instructed to come together immediately and discuss events whenever they are uncomfortable, or unsure of, moving forward.

evidence.quote: The Test Readiness Review (TRR) did not adequately address the configuration of the system under test...test/system configuration to ensure cross checking between drawings applicable to systems being tested
personnel assumed that all 6 of the similar GN2 panels shared the same configuration...4 of the 6 heritage panels had valves, regulators, and gages rather than capped lines

The drawings for the heritage part of the system were created and maintained by an organization different than that designing the new/modified part...multiple organizations with responsibilities in the same facility

impact_notes: Close Call event with potential for oxygen-deficient atmosphere in facility interstitial walls and high bay/airlock areas if disconnected lines or leaks had been present; could have endangered personnel safety.

title: ‘Close Call' with Flooding of the Mars 2020 Rover Testbed
nasa_topics: Environmental Control and Life Support Systems
Long term sustainability and maintenance planning
abstract_text: Flooding of the Mars 2020 project testbed was only averted because an alert engineer observed an indicator light warning that an evaporator overflow pan was about to overflow. Subsequent review revealed a trend of incidents in which JPL building utility anomalies posed a threat to high-value JPL products. Buildings housing critical facilities or hardware should be assessed for their vulnerability to building structural and utility anomalies. New construction should seek to avert such vulnerabilities.
driving_event_text: The NASA/Caltech Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) makes extensive use of high fidelity testbeds, incorporating flight or flight-like hardware and software, for system development and for system-level test with the equipment powered. During mission operations following launch, the testbed also performs an essential role in reproducing and correcting the problems and failures that occur to spacecraft during spaceflight and surface operations. Assuring that a JPL project has adequate components to support extensive testing in the testbed has proven to be a success factor for projects that typically launch only a single spacecraft. Consequently, a system testbed may contain hardware that the project cannot replace. The system testbed for the Mars 2020 rover project is located in a JPL test facility that also houses the testbeds for Mars Science Laboratory (MSL) and Mars Exploration Rover (MER). They share a common humidifier system for treating ambient airflow in the vicinity of the testbeds. At 7:00 am on July 2, 2014, a project engineer observed that a warning indicator light on the humidifier’s secondary containment drip evaporator overflow pan was illuminated, and he immediately covered the hardware on the Mars 2020 testbed with an anti-static plastic sheet. A few minutes later, water streamed onto the covered equipment (Reference (1)). It was quickly determined that the humidifier system strainer was plugged, causing water (instead of steam for humidity control) to spray into the air plenum located in the ceiling and to stream onto the covered equipment (Figure 1). No damage to any testbed equipment occurred. The humidifier alarm is connected to an emergency notification system that alerted the JPL (on duty 24/7) facility maintenance contractor; they responded to the alarm, turned off the water and cleaned the plugged strainers. Subsequent investigation, however, established that the preventative maintenance instructions for the building did not show the location of the strainers; nor did they require periodic cleaning of the strainers, which had never been cleaned (Reference (2)). JPL System Safety (Reference (3)) views this incident as a "close call": if it had occurred late at night or if alert staff had not been present, significant damage to irreplaceable Mars 2020 flight development and flight support hardware would have resulted. In addition, the incident presented a personnel safety slip hazard because the secondary drain routed overflow onto the smooth tile floor directly in front of the access doors to the facility. This incident also added to the impression that the testbed facility has had a trend of anomalies since 2003, including a negative pressure event (Reference (4)) where the air conditioning vents pulled air out instead of blowing it in. (This made it “nearly impossible” to open the outward-hinged doors.) It was discovered after this air conditioning event that (like the building reported in Reference (5) that experienced a flood) the building was not on the JPL critical facility list. References: JPL Mishap Report No. 3087 (Bldg. 317), July 2, 2014. “Water leak on ceiling in RCE lab on 7/2/2014,” JPL Problem/Failure Report No. 56641, July 8, 2014. “July 2014 Testbed Mishap,” e-mail from Matthew Riley, May 11, 2016. “Water leak in CETB lab,” JPL Problem/Failure Report No. 56630, July 7, 2014. “Poor Coordination of Routine Maintenance Spoiled an Important Test,” NEN No. 10401, NASA Lesson Learned Information System (LLIS), August 11, 2014. “Provide Adequate Maintenance and Hazard Response for UPS Units,” NEN No. 12101, NASA Lesson Learned Information System (LLIS), July 14, 2014. JPL Mishap Report No. 0100 (Bldg. 318), November 11, 2002. JPL Mishap Report No. 1995 (Bldg. 306), November 13, 2009. JPL Mishap Report No. 2001 (Bldg. 306), November 24, 2009. JPL Mishap Report No. 2365 (Bldg. 317), February 17, 2011. JPL Mishap Report No. 1822 (Bldg. 179), February 14, 2009. “Ensure Test Monitoring Software Imposes Limits to Prevent Overtest,” NEN No. 1529, NASA Lesson Learned Information System (LLIS), October 11, 2004. “Contamination of the Mars Observer Spacecraft by Environmental Conditioning,” NEN No. 441, NASA Lesson Learned Information System (LLIS), August 23, 1996.
lesson_text: There exists a trend of incidents (see References (5) and (6)) with JPL building utility anomalies posing a threat to JPL products, and the JPL critical facility list does not appear to have been effective in forestalling them. Indeed, ceiling leaks from clogged humidifier or air conditioning drains are fairly commonplace at JPL (References (7) through (10)). For example, in March 2006 (Reference (7)), a wet ceiling tile fell, narrowly missing the Power Processing Unit (PPU) test area for the Dawn project (Figures 1 and 2). Without proactive steps as JPL buildings age, problems like corroded pipes (Reference (11)) and leaky roofs could become more prevalent. JPL facility safety surveys are intended to focus on laboratory-specific issues like ESD protection and use of protective goggles, rather than on building maintenance issues. Figure 2 . General location of DAWN mishap Figure 3 . Configuration of Dawn PPU/ion engine Digital Control & Interface Unit (DCIU) test lab during mishap It is an accepted practice to perform Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) to identify all potential failure modes that could be induced by ground support equipment. For example, the project seeks to assure that the power output of commercial test equipment cannot in any circumstance damage the flight equipment to which it is connected (Reference (12)). Similarly, system safety engineers at the launch site verify that the air conditioning routed to a launch shroud cannot serve as a source of payload contamination (Reference (13)). However, there is a demarcation of responsibility where testbed safety is evaluated by aerospace engineers while building utilities are vouchsafed by building construction and maintenance personnel. For example, FMEA is a specialized reliability engineering task that is not typically performed on building utilities.
recommendation_text: JPL System Safety should identify especially critical buildings where harm to their contents would pose a significant (i.e., possibly irrecoverable) setback to flight projects. (It is not uncommon for JPL facilities to be repurposed for critical tasks or storage that are not reflected on the JPL critical facilities list.) These buildings should be assessed for their vulnerability to a list of common (e.g., leaky roof, power outage) and not-so-common building structural and utility anomalies. Remedial action should be taken consonant with the risk. Plans for new JPL building construction and for major modifications should include features that route water pipes away from areas that may house critical equipment.

evidence.quote: preventative maintenance instructions for the building did not show the location of the strainers; nor did they require periodic cleaning of the strainers, which had never been cleaned
a demarcation of responsibility where testbed safety is evaluated by aerospace engineers while building utilities are vouchsafed by building construction and maintenance personnel
if it had occurred late at night or if alert staff had not been present, significant damage to irreplaceable Mars 2020 flight development and flight support hardware would have resulted

impact_notes: Close call averted only by alert engineer observation. Potential loss of irreplaceable Mars 2020 flight hardware and testbed assets; pattern of similar incidents (humidifier/AC drain clogs, corroded pipes, leaky roofs) at JPL poses recurring risk to high-value flight projects.

title: MSFC Case Study: SLS Test Stand Site Selection, (MSFC-CS1003-1 )
nasa_topics: Requirements critical to costing and cost credibility
Center distribution of programs and management activities
Communications between different offices and contractor personnel
Engineering design and project processes and standards
abstract_text: This case study discusses the challenges faced on the Space Launch System (SLS) program on the site selection for the testing of the core stage assembly. Three sites were selected as options for considerations, each requiring consideration of existing capabilities, siting considerations, and logistical impacts. This case presents an opportunity to discuss infrastructure decision making for large systems. MSFC Case Studies released to the public can be found in NASA Technical Reports System (NTRS)
driving_event_text: Test site selection is a critical element of the design, development and production of a new system. With the advent of the new Space Launch System (SLS), the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) had a number of test site selection decisions that needed to be made early enough in the Program to support the planned Launch Readiness Date (LRD). This case study focuses on decisions that needed to be made in 2011 and 2012 in preparation for the April 2013 DPMC decision about where to execute the Main Propulsion Test that is commonly referred to as “Green Run.” Those decisions relied upon cooperative analysis between the Program, the Test Lab and Center Operations. The Program decided to perform a single green run test on the integrated core stage prior to shipment of it to Kennedy Space Center (KSC) for use in the EM-1 test flight of the SLS vehicle. The SLS Program had to decide where to perform SLS green run testing.
lesson_text: 1. Facilities decisions can require significant upfront planning to ensure production and test capability when and where needed 2. Resources, personal and group access, environmental impacts, Center missions, political impacts, costs, schedule availability, risk, transportation, and infrastructure impacts must be considered as well as test article requirements when selecting a test site.
recommendation_text: 1. Identify options for test site selection considering a broad range of issues such as resources, personal and group access, environmental impacts, Center missions, political impacts, costs, schedule availability, risk, transportation, and infrastructure impacts. 2. Engineers, scientists, and program managers need to work closely with institutional organizations supporting facilities selection and development to address issues that impact institutional elements

evidence.quote: Facilities decisions can require significant upfront planning to ensure production and test capability when and where needed
Resources, personal and group access, environmental impacts, Center missions, political impacts, costs, schedule availability, risk, transportation, and infrastructure impacts must be considered
Engineers, scientists, and program managers need to work closely with institutional organizations supporting facilities selection and development to address issues that impact institutional elements

title: TVAC Lamp Spectrum
abstract_text: The spectrum of the quartz lamps originally used for the Stratospheric Aerosol and Gas Experiment (SAGE) III on the International Space Station (ISS) Instrument Assembly (IA) Thermal VACuum (TVAC) testing did not match the assumed spectrum used in the thermal model. To achieve correlation with the thermal model, a test was performed to characterize the solar flux level within the chamber as well as the chamber thermal gradients. This allowed for an improved correlation of the IA model; however, the additional time and resources required to perform the characterization test and complex model correlation were not ideal. For the Instrument Payload (IP) TVAC test, a heater plate system was designed, built and installed in the facility. This system, coupled with the chamber’s cold shroud, provided the necessary environments for the IP to reach its temperature targets without using the quartz lamps.
driving_event_text: The spectrum of the quartz lamps originally used for the Stratospheric Aerosol and Gas Experiment (SAGE) III on the International Space Station (ISS) Instrument Assembly (IA) Thermal VACuum (TVAC) testing did not match the assumed spectrum used in the thermal model. To achieve correlation with the thermal model, a test was performed to characterize the solar flux level within the chamber as well as the chamber thermal gradients. This allowed for an improved correlation of the IA model; however, the additional time and resources required to perform the characterization test and complex model correlation were not ideal. For the Instrument Payload (IP) TVAC test, a heater plate system was designed, built and installed in the facility. This system, coupled with the chamber’s cold shroud, provided the necessary environments for the IP to reach its temperature targets without using the quartz lamps.
lesson_text: Quartz lamps supply radiant heat largely in the solar spectrum, but the distribution depends upon lamp setting which creates significant complications for model correlation. Correlating results with a thermal model may be easier and more successful by using heater plates rather than developing a thermal model to account for the necessary details of the lamp spectrum.
recommendation_text: If it is desired to correlate thermal models to TVAC test data, it is best to avoid the use of quartz lamps.

evidence.quote: The spectrum of the quartz lamps originally used for the Stratospheric Aerosol and Gas Experiment (SAGE) III on the International Space Station (ISS) Instrument Assembly (IA) Thermal VACuum (TVAC) testing did not match the assumed spectrum
Quartz lamps supply radiant heat largely in the solar spectrum, but the distribution depends upon lamp setting which creates significant complications for model correlation.
If it is desired to correlate thermal models to TVAC test data, it is best to avoid the use of quartz lamps.

impact_notes: Additional time and resources required to perform characterization test and complex model correlation; downstream IP TVAC test required redesign and installation of heater plate system.

title: Preserve End-of-Mission Knowledge
abstract_text: Sol Runner, a collaboration tool used for Mars Phoenix operations, was decommissioned upon project completion. Years later, when the tool contents were needed by another Mars lander project, it was found that a Sol Runner developer had taken the initiative to save a copy on his personal computer, and the data was consequently available for restoration and reuse. The Laboratory culture needs to encourage the initiative to institutionally husband key knowledge.
driving_event_text: A spaceflight project at the NASA/Caltech Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL) generates vast quantities of spacecraft design and operational data. The data is collected and stored in either existing repositories and applications shared across the institution, or in tools specifically developed for the mission. (For example, special tools may be needed to enable collaboration between geographically dispersed operations teams.) Support for such tools may be terminated by a project at certain milestones—spacecraft launch or end-of-mission—when they are no longer needed and/or the project no longer has funds to maintain them. However, this retained knowledge may remain valuable to the institution for reuse on future projects. Whether it resided in a repository or within a mind, restoring lost project knowledge is not only costly and time-consuming, but it imposes additional risk because the original knowledge was proven in ground test and spaceflight. In addition, reflight of JPL flight systems is becoming more common—MSL to M2020; Jason 1, 2 and 3; OCO, OCO-2, and OCO-3. Mars Phoenix was a JPL mission that alighted a stationary lander in the Martian polar region in May 2008; it successfully completed its mission in August 2008. During this period, the Mars Phoenix operations team employed a JPL-developed software tool named Sol Runner that resided on a computer located on the JPL campus. Sol Runner was a collaborative tool that enabled a mission operations team monitoring a lander subsystem to post reports and subsystem status data. This product was intended for view not only by the subsystem team members, but it was also used for handover to the next shift and used by the other subsystem and system teams who based decisions on the subsystem status. This single source of truth helped make a complex, dispersed, and time-consuming daily mission operations planning process (Figure 1) more manageable. Figure 1. The Mars Phoenix operations process required the teams to work 14-hour shifts on “Mars time” Although the cold temperatures and reduced sunlight associated with the onset of the Martian winter effectively ended the mission (as expected) in 2008, JPL entertained the possibility that the Martian spring might warm the lander batteries. When it was clear that communications with the lander could not be restored, the project was formally ended in May 2010, and project resources like Sol Runner that weren’t thought to be needed for another JPL project were decommissioned or scrapped. Three years later, operations planning was underway for a future Mars lander that was based on the successful Mars Phoenix design. The JPL InSight (Interior Exploration using Seismic Investigations, Geodesy and Heat Transport) project has been approved to place a geophysics lander on Mars to study its deep interior. In August 2013, an InSight software systems architect sought some of the captured knowledge from the Phoenix mission that had been stored in Sol Runner. He contacted one of the Sol Runner developers, who informed him that the tool had been decommissioned. Such decommissioning is in accordance with JPL project close-out guidance (Reference (1)). However, the developer also reported that he had, on his own initiative, made a complete copy of the Sol Runner system before it was deactivated. The copy included the code and user-uploaded attachments, plus the entire set of database entries. He had copied all this data to his laptop because he felt it might someday be needed. The Sol Runner developer restored the Mars Phoenix content and provided it to the InSight engineers and scientists for reuse in planning InSight mission operations. References: Paragraph 5.29: “Project Closeout,” JPL Flight Project Practices (Rev. 11), JPL DocID 58032, January 4, 2017. Paragraph 5.11, "Information Management," JPL Flight Project Practices (Rev. 11), JPL DocID 58032, January 4, 2017.
lesson_text: Follow-on projects with significant heritage content (e.g., the Mars 2020 design is based on that of Mars Science Laboratory) are increasingly common at JPL. Yet, institutions commonly act as if the knowledge gained during the lifespan of a project has little or no residual value past the end of the project. Even when the specific systems (i.e., the hardware and software) are not inherited from a previous project, JPL is always building upon its knowledge of how to design spacecraft autonomy, perform Entry, Descent, and Landing (EDL), etc. If JPL were not funded to develop another Mars lander every 5 or 10 years, it is not clear that JPL could maintain its knowledge of how to perform EDL. Loss of critical knowledge has become a more acute problem due to turnover related to the aging of the NASA and JPL workforce. Project tools developed and maintained by JPL, as well as subscribed commercial tools obtained under a license, pose monthly maintenance costs. For example, the license for the commercial platform JPL uses to host project wikis costs about $30 per month. Although JPL recognizes the residual value of a wiki’s content after project completion, JPL lacks a mechanism to fund the $30 after the project accounts are closed.
recommendation_text: In response to a 2012 Agency initiative, JPL has established a knowledge management program to capture, retain, and share key JPL knowledge for reuse. The program supports such activities as lessons learned capture, enhanced search technology, Pause & Learn sessions, wiki development, and storytelling. But because progress requires some cultural change, the line and project organizations need to actively promote knowledge husbandry. For example, an employee must be encouraged to save a newly completed, important study to somewhere other than just their computer drive so it can be accessed by someone who may need the knowledge. JPL projects are required to archive project records no later than the project closeout that occurs near and after the end of mission (Reference (2)). The JPL program that archives each completed project’s electronic library should be expanded in recognition of the increased JPL use of tools other than content management systems (CMSs)—wikis, communities of practice, internally-built tools such as Sol Runner, etc. JPL personnel’s awareness of read/write knowledge networks like the JPL Tube searchable video library, the JPL Wired wiki, Spark, and the NASA Lesson Learned Information System (LLIS) should be enhanced (Reference (2)).

evidence.quote: JPL lacks a mechanism to fund the $30 after the project accounts are closed...institutions commonly act as if the knowledge gained during the lifespan of a project has little or no residual value past the end of the project.
Sol Runner...was decommissioned upon project completion...a Sol Runner developer had taken the initiative to save a copy on his personal computer
JPL lacks a mechanism to fund the $30 after the project accounts are closed...JPL personnel's awareness of read/write knowledge networks...should be enhanced

impact_notes: Loss of critical operational knowledge from Mars Phoenix Sol Runner tool; institutional risk to follow-on projects (Mars 2020, MSL heritage systems) due to knowledge gaps and inability to maintain project tools post-mission; workforce turnover compounds knowledge loss risk.

title: Employee Falls Down Steep Ramp at Door without a Landing
abstract_text: Non-Code building egress/ingress ramp results in permanent-partial injury
driving_event_text: An employee attempted to enter an older facility using an entrance that has a steep ramp without a landing at the doorway. After ascending the ramp the employee grasped the door handle and pulled. Unfortunately, the door flexed and rebounded causing the employee to lose balance and fall down the ramp. Upon falling, the employee’s head struck the pavement of the adjacent parking lot and suffered a permanent-partial injury. Upon investigation it was found that the ramp leading into the facility had a slope of 1:6.27 (9 feet 5 inches run with a drop of 1 feet 6 inches) which exceeds the California Building Code for Historical Buildings (Accessibility Section, 8-603.6). In addition, it was found that the egress/ingress way had no handrails and it and the grounds outside the building were covered with leaves which impaired footing and visibility. Figure 1: Photo of doorway after mitigations
lesson_text: Egress/ingress ramps without doorway landings and handrails should not be used.
recommendation_text: Supervisors and Facility Managers should ensure that employees have a safe means of building egress/ingress by conducting periodic inspections and closing off passages that are unsafe until corrective actions are implemented. All employees should refrain from using unsafe building egress/ingress passages and report them to their supervisor and/or facility manager.

evidence.quote: ramp leading into the facility had a slope of 1:6.27...which exceeds the California Building Code for Historical Buildings...no handrails
egress/ingress way...grounds outside the building were covered with leaves which impaired footing and visibility
Supervisors and Facility Managers should ensure that employees have a safe means of building egress/ingress by conducting periodic inspections

impact_notes: Permanent-partial injury to employee from head strike; unsafe facility infrastructure defect.

title: Decision Making and Support Processes Enabling Rapid Testing
program_relation: Exploration Ground Systems (EGS) Program at Kennedy Space Center (KSC)
abstract_text: The Exploration Ground Systems (EGS) Program at Kennedy Space Center (KSC) experienced issues in the early phases of testing of Ground Support Equipment and other facility and support systems. These issues were related to: 1. test team having common understanding of individual roles and responsibilities; 2. Communicating problems and test status; 3. Processes used to integrate and coordinate work spanning multiple organizations; and 4. quickly dispositioning and resolving issues and returning to testing. The results of these issues included testing delays and cost growth. When performing large-scale complex testing it is important to ensure that equipment and supporting services are available before testing is initiated, roles and responsibilities are clearly understood, that the right stakeholders are involved in technical discussions and decisions, that problem resolutions and other technical decisions are made quickly and communicated clearly and that the process supports quickly returning to testing.
driving_event_text: The EGS Program performed subsystem-level and integrated verification and validation (V&V) testing during its design and development phase leading up to Exploration Mission 1. Umbilicals and launch accessories were tested at the Launch Equipment Test Facility. Other tests were performed where systems were deployed in various facilities. Completion of tests was delayed and the budget allocated for the testing was exceeded. Testing was at times delayed due to spare parts being unavailable, services (e.g. calibration) not being able to support the testing schedule, and delays in correcting issues encountered during testing and quickly returning to testing. Figure 1: Umbilical testing at the Launch Equiptment Test Facility (LETF)
lesson_text: In situations where complex testing must be completed quickly, V&V planning should include a method for rapidly identifying and dispositioning issues encountered during testing, modifying hardware, software and documentation and resuming testing. Testing processes need to support efficient Problem Report/Nonconformance disposition process. The process needs to be supported by on-site capability for modifying hardware/software and quickly updating the engineering documentation and testing procedures. Delays during V&V testing in facilities where unmodified heritage systems had a long period without maintenance resulted from uncertainty about the condition of unmaintained systems. Bringing the unmaintained systems back into a usable state was time-consuming. It is important to ensure that all equipment is ready for use before any testing involving that equipment. EGS Elements did not always purchase spares ahead of time to support V&V testing. Some of these spares were long lead items. Late procurement of long-lead spares resulted in testing delays. Spares were purchased in small groups rather than large orders and were more expensive. It is important to ensure spares are available to support V&V testing, especially long-lead items. When doing large-scale testing, it is important to identify the required services and commodities ahead of time so that institutional suppliers can provide them in a timely manner. KSC uses fixed-price contracting for institutional support providers and these commodities and services may become much more expensive if the quantity of the service or commodity are outside the baseline contract scope. An example of a support service we had issues with is calibration. Test scheduling did not account for delays caused by the large number of calibrations needed to support testing. Work being performed in KSC facilities is complex due to the responsibility for various systems being divided between a numbers of contracts. When projects modify systems in these facilities, there may be other organizations in these facilities doing work at the same time. Modifying one system may impact systems owned by different organizations. The IT contractor, the facility support contractor, various EGS projects and the installation contractors for hardware may all be trying to operate simultaneously in the facility. Coordinating activities within the facility across all the organizations and coordinating and communicating system outages with all stakeholders is vital to success. For example, an outage for modifications to Ground Special Power may affect maintenance or testing activities involving other systems.
recommendation_text: Testing process: Ensure that test team roles and responsibilities are clearly defined and understood by the test team. Ensure that technical discussions leading up to a decision involve the decision-makers so that decision-makers understand the reasons behind recommended solutions. Ensure that the process supports a rapid return to testing after a decision is made. Support testing process by providing capability to rapidly modify hardware and software, update documentation and test procedures. Ensure any unmaintained heritage systems are ready to support upcoming testing prior to initiation of testing Procure V&V spares, particularly long-lead items ahead of time Ensure there is a thorough and well-understood process for coordinating work being performed by different organizations or teams in an area. This should include a process for vetting and approving system outages that may impact other teams. Identify when and what quantities of commodities and services (e.g. calibration) are required for testing. Ensure providers can deliver the quantity and type of commodity or service when and where they are needed to support testing.

evidence.quote: V&V planning should include a method for rapidly identifying and dispositioning issues encountered during testing...Testing processes need to support efficient Problem Report/Nonconformance disposition process.
Late procurement of long-lead spares resulted in testing delays. Spares were purchased in small groups rather than large orders and were more expensive.
Coordinating activities within the facility across all the organizations and coordinating and communicating system outages with all stakeholders is vital to success.

impact_notes: Testing delays and budget cost growth due to inadequate V&V planning, spare parts procurement, service coordination, and multi-organization facility work integration.

title: System / Facility Impedance Checks
program_relation: Stratospheric and Aerosol Gas Experiment III on the International Space Station (SAGEIII on ISS)
abstract_text: At the start of Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) / Electromagnetic Compatibility (EMC) testing for the Stratospheric and Aerosol Gas Experiment III on the International Space Station (SAGE III on ISS) Interface Adapter Module (IAM), the IAM suffered a failure that resulted in the disassembly, troubleshooting, and reassembly of the flight hardware. Investigation revealed that an impedance compatibility problem existed between the facility and the IAM. This resulted in the supply voltage oscillating to the point of damaging the IAM.
driving_event_text: Figure 1. Interface Adapter Module (IAM), Contamination Monitoring Packages (CMPs), and Disturbance Monitoring Package (DMP) test photo The SAGE III on ISS IAM, Contamination Monitoring Packages (CMPs), and Disturbance Monitoring Package (DMP) were prepared for testing in the LaRC Building 1220 EMC facility (see Figure 1). During pre-EMI functional testing, noise was observed on the Power Distribution Unit (PDU) 28V monitors (see Figure 2). Troubleshooting tests continued at the facility until two bus fuses were blown. Figure 2. Voltage and Current Oscillations about the IAM PDU (blue) and the DMP (red) The LaRC EMC facility, and many other facilities, use in-wall filters for the Ground Support Equipment (GSE) power delivery. This provides EMC test signal isolation and noise rejection for the GSE power delivery. Configuration 2, used in much of the testing, is shown schematically in Figure 3. Figure 3. Ringing Test Setup for Configuration 2 In this configuration, there is no bulk capacitance between the power feeds on the chamber side of the facility wall filters. The illustrated chamber-side table capacitors are relatively small, 10 microF. This satisfies a specified facility configuration requirement for ISS electromagnetic testing techniques. During troubleshooting, the IAM test team conducted some simple ringing tests on the EMC power system as it was operated during the IAM failure anomaly. With step load current changes, appreciable ringing was observed in the 600 – 700 Hz range. This seemed to correlate to other load changes in the measured IAM data. An incompatibility between the as-tested EMC power system configuration and the flight hardware was postulated. A measurement of the output impedance of the facility was set up as shown in the schematic of Figure 4. Figure 4. Impedance measurement test setup. The tool used to measure impedance is the Venable VT350c. The system calculates impedance by measuring voltage at the device divided by current into the device, including phase, where device is either the load or the power system. To do so across frequency, it applies a small voltage oscillation, coupled via the pictured transformer into the supply lead, and observes the resultant voltage and current oscillations. Iteratively, it steps across the specified frequency range to produce impedance vs frequency, both magnitude and phase. The result of the configuration #2 impedance sweep is shown below. On the left axis is the magnitude (blue) and on the right is the phase (red). Light red and blue is a plot of the load impedance applied to the EMC power system during this test, a 70-Ohm resistive load for this example. The flatness and accuracy of the plot confirms the Venable system’s operation. The measurements above 100 kHz are less valid because this is nearer the limits of the Venable system and at these frequencies, the test setup wiring starts to be part of the measurement. Later testing with the Venable system is changed to end at 100 kHz to avoid any confusion and improve testing efficiency. Figure 5. Impedance measurements of Configuration 2. The darker traces in Figure 5 are the plots of interest. The dark red is the magnitude and the dark blue is the phase of the output impedance of the EMC power system during the IAM anomaly, Configuration 2. There are several critical features. First, the magnitude of the output impedance from 100 Hz to 10 kHz is very high, above 1 Ohm generally, quickly peaking to 33 Ohms at 660 Hz. This 660 Hz peak corresponds to the frequency of oscillation in the ringing tests. A second peak of 10 Ohms is at 2 kHz. The magnitude of the output impedance shown above would easily prove incompatible with the flight hardware system. As a result of the impedance incompatibility, the facility baseline configuration was adjusted to include a 10,000 microF capacitor in series with a 0.3 Ohm resistance across the power supply and return lines. The new baseline configuration set up for measuring impedance is illustrated in Figure 6. Figure 6. New Baseline Configuration Impedance Measurement Setup Output impedance of the EMC new baseline configuration was measured and is shown in Figure 7. The plot shows that the impedance is very flat, both in magnitude and phase. The worst-case magnitude is less than 0.5 Ohms. Additional testing with setups appropriate for selected other Conducted Susceptibility (CS) and Conducted Emissions (CE) tests showed the configuration with the 10,000 microF capacitor in series with a 0.3 Ohm resistance across the power supply and return lines to produce appropriately low and flat output impedances. Figure 7. Output Impedance of New Baseline Power Delivery System In addition to the inclusion of the capacitor-resistor, facility process changes were implemented to reduce the chances for future testing problems. In the past, power supplies and much of the cabling was supplied by the respective projects. This produced an unknown in testing that could be eliminated by having standard facility power supply and cables, thereby allowing the EMC test facility to characterize the EMC Power Delivery System for different loads, voltages, and test configurations. The EMC facility power wiring were placed under configuration control and wiring is inspected. The power rack and cabling have a logbook to hold schematics, document any changesto the configuration, and provide for quality inspection. Where generic cables with crimp terminals need to be used, qualified tooling has been used and pull tests conducted successfully and inspected. The EMC facility will now prefer that projects consider their input impedance combined with the facility-provided EMC Power Delivery System output impedance in all test configurations.
lesson_text: Interplay between Equipment Under Test (EUT) and a testing facility can result in unexpected system behavior that can lead to unanticipated test results and even EUT failure.
recommendation_text: In general, the output impedance of any power delivery system should be known and compared against the input impedance of any load to ensure the power delivery will be stable and as expected. Middlebrook is a good stability criterion but is very conservative. Gain Margin Phase Margin is less conservative, but the results are stable. Engineering judgement should be used to determine to what extent the analysis is completed. For EUT that is considered to be of high value (either from a monetary or programmatic perspective), those responsible for the EUT should measure the input impedance and perform appropriate analyses to confirm that the entire test configuration will be stable. For EUT of lower value, e.g., hardware from bench top research projects, assumptions about the lower output impedance of supplies can be successfully made, but the concept of power delivery stability, especially as electrical power designs begin to daisy-chain more DC/DC conversion, should always be remembered.

evidence.quote: During pre-EMI functional testing, noise was observed on the Power Distribution Unit (PDU) 28V monitors...troubleshooting tests continued at the facility until two bus fuses were blown.

An incompatibility between the as-tested EMC power system configuration and the flight hardware was postulated...there is no bulk capacitance between the power feeds on the chamber side of the facility wall filters.
Interplay between Equipment Under Test (EUT) and a testing facility can result in unexpected system behavior that can lead to unanticipated test results and even EUT failure.

impact_notes: Flight hardware (IAM) suffered damage requiring disassembly, troubleshooting, and reassembly during EMI/EMC testing; two bus fuses blown; schedule and resource impact from unplanned rework.

title: Utility Energy Services Contract Lessons Learned
abstract_text: Lessons learned were collected during a project to provide utility services at the Kennedy Space Center (KSC) in six major areas; Communication, Documentation, Project Requirements, Cost/ Finance, Organization and Technical Reviews.
driving_event_text: In December 2020, a project to provide utility services at the Kennedy Space Center (KSC) was initiated. The Utility Energy Services Contract scope covered 22 facilities with mechanical, electrical, HVAC and water as well as street lighting and 2-megawatt (MW) solar fields. Lessons learned were collected in six major areas: Communication, Documentation, Project Requirements, Cost/ Finance, Organization and Technical Reviews.
lesson_text: The Lessons Learned are divided into six major categories: Communication Ensure Facility Managers are aware of scope and timeline Keep stakeholders updated and aware of progress Establish prime stakeholder meeting frequency Utilize Gantt chart where possible to project path and communicate with stakeholders Documentation Compile the 1509 draft early on in the process Ensure proper UESC project title Compile Special Conditions document draft early Ensure demo list is updated Properly document legal requirements and the NASA process Project Requirements Determine speed needed as a larger scope tends to take much longer to accomplish Cost/ Finance Determine need for Basis of Estimate from contractor and acquire Determine support costs for planning Ensure finance terms and review Points of Contact (POC)s are defined and followed Organization Determine full POC list as soon as possible Determine stakeholders and responsibilities early Technical Reviews Ensure review cycles are established Determine need for Tech Eval
recommendation_text: Organization Title Driving Event Lesson(s) Learned Recommendation Determine full POC list as soon as possible Saves time alarming folks ahead of time to prevent restarting process of sharing with each group later Have people involved earlier in the process (need specifics) Perform a stakeholder awareness meeting as soon as possible and reconvene at each phase (Preliminary Assessment, Feasibility Study, etc.) Determine stakeholders and responsibilities early Decide on which parties to manage the UESC and which phases, such as Preliminary Assessment, Feasibility Study, and Design and Implementation Procurement Officer may need to be delegated more responsibility May need to consider benefits and ramifications Cost/ Finance Title Driving Event Lesson(s) Learned Recommendation Determine support costs for planning Essential for budgeting, planning, and the PPBE cycle(s) Know contingency and BOSS / NEMCON support activities and approximate cost KSC estimates plan ~6% for SEIS/Shop support, ~2% Environmental, ~10% Contingency Ensure Finance terms and review POCs are defined and followed Prevents committal to non-ideal terms or higher expense to NASA over life of loan Finance elements: have full list of requirements from lenders reviewed by finance and procurement prior to ESCO (FPL) engaging with lenders. Establish a proper POC list of stakeholders for making a decision (KSC: finance, procurement, legal) and communicate timeline for decision need (5 business days) Determine need for Basis of Estimate from contractor and acquire For Sole Sourcing - "Certificate for Current Cost and Pricing," employees will work on job. If labor rates are less, CO can sign a "Certificate of Defective Pricing" asking for money back. Need to get Basis of Estimate from contractor to save time in the long run Determine need for Basis of Estimate from contractor and acquire Communication Title Driving Event Lesson(s) Learned Recommendation Keep stakeholders updated and aware of progress Ensure scope is tracked to prevent duplication of work by other groups and keep customers/stakeholders aware of status Customer engagement: continue to engage at every stage of UESC with each review cycle to maintain awareness Beneficial to have all programs in same meeting vs separate meetings for each program Ensure Facility Managers are aware of scope and timeline Program managers may not be aware of information that Facility Managers have that could contribute to project success Ensure every facility in scope has Facility Manager (FM) identified throughout process Alt could be Program Manager if no FM is available; however, engage at beginning of Feasibility Study and at 100% at minimum Utilize Gantt chart where possible to project path and communicate with stakeholders Non-essential delays are incurred in determining next steps Determine if potential parallel activities can be done Scope compiled, Construction of Facilities (CoF) to complete actions, programs to review and approve Establish prime stakeholder meeting frequency Without constant communication with stakeholder groups, project tends to lose focus and momentum as well as ease in resolving problems that pop up More frequent meetings with GG and programs/owners of facilities to avoid late game changes with programs KSC has seen success in a half-hour weekly tag up on UESC Project Requirements Title Driving Event Lesson(s) Learned Recommendation Determine speed needed as larger scope tends to take much longer to accomplish KSC's UESC Phase 1 Feasibility Study took ~2 years to accomplish due to significant size of scope and POC's involved Benefit of having UESC limited to single program vs a multi-program effort Split UESC based on scope type (i.e., Lighting) or by Program (i.e., ISS) Compile Special Conditions document draft early Special Conditions document discussions and decisions can take a decent time to complete; desire is to not have this as a long-lead event extending timeline Special Conditions document to be started earlier -- at possibly 90% stage rather than Tech Eval Seek template from EEP or prior center project template to copy and paste Technical Reviews Title Driving Event Lesson(s) Learned Recommendation Determine need for Tech Eval Tech evaluations take time and can become challenging if long periods of time exist between review cycles Potentially limit Tech Eval to once every ‘x’ months Recommend having at 90%, 100%, and Final only Ensure review cycles are established Prevents extra, unnecessary reviews and potential additional incurred costs for Utility and/or government Include in contract how many review cycles to include to avoid incurring an additional cycle A 45%, 90%, 100%, Final, with no additional updates to Final submittal Documentation Title Driving Event Lesson(s) Learned Recommendation Compile 1509 draft early 1509 reviews can take up to 3 weeks, delaying project from moving forward if not planned properly 1509 actions – ensure payback verbiage is well documented and available for response Meet with local center and NASA HQ POCs to ensure process and data are drafted correctly and reviewed, which would expedite final 1509/1510 submittal Ensure proper UESC project title From discussing the KSC named "UESC Phase 1" effort became confusing with discussion with NREL, NASA HQ, and CoF as "phase" can mean many different things Project naming convention needs consistency and clarity as “Phases” tend to be confusing since the term can imply Feasibility Study vs Preliminary Assessment or others Name effort based on project category or program (example: Lighting, ISS) Ensure demo list is updated Pursued scope in facility soon to be demonstrated Saves time investing in facilities soon to be demolished Engage with Facilities Division and Demo POC during each phase of UESC Properly document legal requirements and NASA process Without prepared response to common questions, time is wasted in researching responses, or worse, not following protocol Figure out how to eliminate past impressions of UESC (example: 10-year ‘legal’ limit vs preferences on payback) Utilize working groups to raise awareness of future intent that is not based on past projects

evidence.quote: Compile the 1509 draft early on in the process...1509 reviews can take up to 3 weeks, delaying project from moving forward if not planned properly
Determine full POC list as soon as possible...Determine stakeholders and responsibilities early
UESC Phase 1 Feasibility Study took ~2 years to accomplish due to significant size of scope and POC's involved

impact_notes: Multi-year schedule delays (Phase 1 took ~2 years); cost impacts from extended review cycles, contingency planning (~10% contingency noted), and rework due to unclear scope and stakeholder misalignment across 22 facilities.

title: E-1 Triethyl Aluminum-Triethyl Borane (TEA-TEB) System Contamination
abstract_text: On June 28th, 2022, during post test inspections, test operations personnel noted that the E-1 Cell 2 Triethyl Aluminum-Triethyl Borane (TEA-TEB) injection supply tubing was warm to the touch. This indicated that there could have been a TEA-TEB reaction occurring inside the tubing. Personnel identified the source of air was coming from FireX Master Facility Panel (MFP) Station 6. No personal injury or facility damage was sustained. Background The FireX MFP Station 6 was originally designed and installed to service valve actuation of all stand FireX systems, and as such the system is branched and routed throughout the stand and all three E-1 test cell locations. TEA-TEB is a pyrophoric liquid which ignites when exposed to air and water, and is often used as an ignition method for rocket testing. It is stable when stored and operated with dry and inert purges until ready to be injected into the rocket components for ignition. The TEA-TEB storage system and run system had been installed for a previous test project in Cell 1 in 2016. The design tapped into a nearby GN system downstream from MFP Station 6 for the required GN purge and pressurization. At this time, MFP drawing and panel labeling was not changed to reflect the additional purpose of Station 6 for TEA-TEB purging. Configuration Control of Inactive Systems In preparation for inclement weather in September 2021, the FireX MFP supply was swapped from gaseous nitrogen to high pressure air (HPA) to conserve nitrogen usage at E-1 and had remained in this configuration ever since. The TEA-TEB system was inactive at the time of the configuration change as the Cell 1 project had concluded and the upcoming Cell 2 project was still in the design/build phase. For this new project, a new TEA-TEB run system was added to Cell 2 in 2022 and was designed to utilize the same TEA-TEB storage system and GN source as the Cell 1 system.
driving_event_text: Root Cause In preparation for inclement weather in September 2021, the FireX MFP supply was swapped from gaseous nitrogen to high pressure air to conserve nitrogen usage at E1. The FireX MFP remained in HPA service post hurricane season to continue to conserve nitrogen usage. E-1 FireX Master Facility Panel Station 6 is used to purge the TEA-TEB. By using this purge/press system with the HPA supply, air was introduced into the TEA-TEB system. Contributing Factors: The Station 6 description did not state that Station 6 was serving as a purge to the TEA-TEB System. Test Operations made a time-critical decision to respond to a stressful situation due to limited deliveries of Liquid Nitrogen to the High-Pressure Gas Facility post Hurricane Ida. The implementation proceeded as a permanent change instead of a temporary change. This did not allow the Test Operations Team a chance to fully review the system configuration without the stress of limited GN and post major hurricane.
lesson_text: Corrective Actions and Recommendations The air source was removed, and the MFP drawing was labeled for “GN service only” and to indicate that Station 6 supplies the TEA-TEB Cart Pressurization and Purge. These labels were also physically added to the MFP Station 6 panel for operators in the field. In addition, a label was added to the TEA-TEB cart door indicating that GN source pressure was routed from MFP Station 6. Ideally, the team would like to fabricate and install a separate and dedicated GN-only station exclusively for the TEA-TEB system. Some other long term actions are being considered for further action. Infrared cameras could be utilized to detect thermal heating in TEA-TEB system tubing, enabling the test conductor to monitor system conditions prior to releasing personnel back to the test stand facility. This capability is already used in other areas, particularly on hydrogen systems. The team is also exploring ways to mistake proof the design and configuration control processes, to use automated systems to identify and flag systems which have commodity restrictions. SEE ATTACHED DOCUMENT
recommendation_text: Ensure proper labeling of system source panels, and especially pay attention to restrictions of commodity sources to avoid unintentional contamination. Always use processes which establish constraints or required verifications prior to reactivating systems which have been inactive for a period of time.

evidence.quote: In preparation for inclement weather in September 2021, the FireX MFP supply was swapped from gaseous nitrogen to high pressure air...The FireX MFP remained in HPA service post hurricane season...By using this purge/press system with the HP

The TEA-TEB system was inactive at the time of the configuration change...At this time, MFP drawing and panel labeling was not changed to reflect the additional purpose of Station 6 for TEA-TEB purging.

The implementation proceeded as a permanent change instead of a temporary change. This did not allow the Test Operations Team a chance to fully review the system configuration without the stress of limited GN and post major hurricane.

impact_notes: Pyrophoric TEA-TEB contamination with air created ignition hazard during test operations. No injury or facility damage occurred, but incident exposed critical safety gap in configuration control of inactive systems with commodity restrictions.

title: Chilled Water Line Failure Under Facility Concrete Slab Resulted In Over $1.5M In Facility Damages
nasa_topics: Engineering design and project processes and standards
abstract_text: At the Kennedy Space Center, the Operations Support Building 1 (OSB 1) had an 8-inch PVC chilled water return pipe catastrophically fail under the facility while operating within the manufacturers allowable pressure rating. A failure analysis of the pipe concluded the pipe elbow was improperly glued together when it was originally installed 40 years ago. In order to prevent a reoccurrence, it is recommended PVC pipe not be used either outside or under facilities for chilled water systems. Allowable other materials are either steel, polypropylene or copper pipe.
driving_event_text: The Kennedy Space Center ​Operations Support Building (OSB) 1 experienced a catastrophic failure of the PVC chilled water return line which ruptured the concrete floors in the southwest corner of the building and flooded a portion of the first floor. The chilled water campus loop had been leaking for years and multiple attempts to find the source of the leak were not successful, because the source of the leak was an elbow under the facility. Personnel in the damaged area of the facility evacuated. The catastrophic failure resulted in a chilled water plant shutdown due to water loss in the system. This resulted in a loss of cooling to all the facilities that are supplied by the chilled water plant including the Launch Control Center and other essential buildings. A Mishap Investigation Team documented the findings in NMIS Case Number: 22-101302.
lesson_text: Chilled water pipe should not be routed under facilities because it can't be inspected, and long term, is subject to stresses induced from settling foundations and shifting soils.
recommendation_text: PVC should not be used for chilled water piping either outside, inside or under facilities. Do not allow the use of PVC piping for chilled water lines inside or outside facilities. Do not allow chilled water piping to be routed under floors/concrete inside facilities. SpecsIntact Master specification 33 61 14 section 2.2.3 and 2.2.4 currently allows for Plastic Reinforced Thermosetting Resin (RTR) and PVC piping in chilled water systems outside facilities. SpecsIntact Master specification 23 64 26 section 1.7.3 Accessibility requires pipes be accessible inside facilities. A clarifying statement is chilled water pipe should not be routed under facilities because it can't be inspected, and long term, is subject to stresses induced from settling foundations and shifting soils.

evidence.quote: the pipe elbow was improperly glued together when it was originally installed 40 years ago
chilled water pipe should not be routed under facilities because it can't be inspected, and long term, is subject to stresses induced from settling foundations and shifting soils
The chilled water campus loop had been leaking for years and multiple attempts to find the source of the leak were not successful, because the source of the leak was an elbow under the facility

impact_notes: Over $1.5M in facility damages; catastrophic chilled water system failure causing loss of cooling to Launch Control Center and other essential buildings; personnel evacuation required.
